figures.caption: Figure 3. Resolution generalisation for Gaussian blob sampling. Models are trained on a coarse triangular mesh with 2048 elements (top) and evaluated on a finer mesh with 8192 elements (bottom). We compare ground truth, CNN, FNO, GAOT, message passing GNN, and the proposed GRIFDIR. Resolution-dependent models degrade or produce artifacts at higher resolution, while resolution-invariant operators maintain spatial structure and sample quality. Samples are generated using 400 diffusion steps with a Heun sampler.
Figure 10. Unconditional samples of the pinball model. See Figure 8 for samples from the dataset. This figure is using the same colour bar with min=0 and max=3.
Figure 4. Unconditional samples of the multi domain model.
Figure 5. Sparse sensor reconstruction for Fun-DPS and FunDAPS with 10, 50 and 100 randomly placed sensors. We show a single sample per setting. Corresponding errors are given in Table 6.
Figure 12. The mean ℓ2-error of the conditional pinball model over the rollout time t.

Figure 7. Sparse sensor reconstruction with 75 randomly placed sensors with Fun-DPS and Fun-DAPS for the pinball dataset. For each method, we show the ground truth, posterior mean and standard deviation (computed with 100 samples), and three posterior samples.
Table 4. Gaussian-blob conductivity datasets: total samples (⌈0.9N⌉ assigned to train at runtime) and node counts at each hierarchy level N0, N1, N2, N3 (finest to coarsest).
Figure 8. Two rollouts of the pinball dataset for different velocities µ from the training set.
Figure 9. The pinball mesh for different length scales and number of elements N .
